rationale: The page provides detailed information on two distinct animal species, Dhole (Asiatic Wild Dog) and Alpine Musk Deer. For each species, it covers context, habitat, characteristics, and conservation status (WPA, CITES, IUCN). The Alpine Musk Deer section also includes a specific threat. This content is highly informative and suitable for generating multiple questions.
cleanedText:
Dhole (Asiatic Wild Dog)
Context:
Dhole, once thought extinct in the region, has made a comeback in Assam's Kaziranga-Karbi Anglong Landscape (KKAL).
Habitat:
India, Cambodia, Nepal, Bhutan, Thailand, Central Asia, etc.
Inhabits Eastern & Western Ghats, Assam, Meghalaya, West Bengal, Ladakh etc.
Characteristics:
Carnivores, resembles small wolf or domestic dog in size, hunt mainly in early morning or late evening.
Conservation Status:
WPA, 1972: Schedule I
CITES: Appendix II
IUCN Status: EN (Endangered)
Alpine Musk Deer
Context:
Central Zoo Authority (CZA) report revealed a mix-up in identifying Alpine musk deer for conservation breeding, mistaking it for its Himalayan counterpart.
Habitat:
Temperate climate in alpine meadows, shrub lands, birch, blue pine and fir forests.
Native species of India, Nepal, Bhutan and China.
Characteristics:
Solitary and shy, herbivorous.
Unique with fangs and without antlers and gall bladder.
Threat:
Poaching for muskpod used in perfumes and traditional medicines.
Conservation Status:
WPA, 1972: Schedule I
CITES: Appendix I
IUCN Status: EN (Endangered)
